document: Reconnaissance. Attacker objective: The adversary is gathering information to plan future operations — identifying targets, mapping networks, and discovering services, accounts, or configurations that can be exploited. Network context: In network telemetry, reconnaissance manifests as the adversary's effort to map the target environment before committing to an attack. Traffic associated with this tactic involves attempts to identify reachable hosts, available services, and exposed information-disclosure interfaces. Representative ATT&CK techniques: T1595 Active Scanning (probing IP ranges and ports); T1592 Gather Victim Host Information (fingerprinting OS/services); T1590 Gather Victim Network Information (mapping topology, DNS records); T1589 Gather Victim Identity Information (harvesting accounts/emails). Kill chain relationships: Reconnaissance is typically the first phase. Successful reconnaissance informs Resource Development and Initial Access — the adversary uses what it learned to craft exploits, phishing, or credential attacks.

Resource Development. Attacker objective: The adversary is establishing resources to support operations — acquiring infrastructure (servers, domains), developing tools, or obtaining credentials and certificates for later use. Network context: Resource Development rarely produces direct network artifacts visible in a target's Zeek logs, since it occurs on the adversary's own infrastructure. Indirect signs may include newly registered domains appearing in DNS queries or TLS certificates from recently provisioned hosts. Representative ATT&CK techniques: T1583 Acquire Infrastructure (buying/renting servers, domains); T1587 Develop Capabilities (creating custom malware/exploits); T1586 Compromise Accounts (taking over third-party accounts); T1588 Obtain Capabilities (downloading tools, purchasing exploits). Kill chain relationships: Resource Development follows Reconnaissance and precedes Initial Access. The adversary prepares the tools and infrastructure needed to gain a foothold in the target environment.

Initial Access. Attacker objective: The adversary is trying to get into the target network — establishing a first foothold through an externally accessible service, credential, or delivery mechanism. Network context: In network telemetry this tactic is associated with inbound connections to Internet-facing services, exploitation traffic targeting known vulnerabilities, and successful authentication from unexpected sources or at unusual times. Representative ATT&CK techniques: T1190 Exploit Public-Facing Application (exploiting web apps, admin panels); T1078 Valid Accounts (using stolen/default credentials); T1133 External Remote Services (VPN, RDP, SSH from outside); T1566 Phishing (delivery of malicious payloads via email/links). Kill chain relationships: Initial Access follows Reconnaissance and Resource Development. A successful foothold enables Execution, Persistence, and further progression through the kill chain.

Execution. Attacker objective: The adversary is trying to run malicious code on the target system — executing payloads, scripts, or commands to achieve their objectives. Network context: In network telemetry, execution itself is often invisible (it happens on the endpoint). Indirect network signs include outbound connections initiated immediately after inbound exploitation, download of additional payloads, or communication with staging infrastructure. Representative ATT&CK techniques: T1059 Command and Scripting Interpreter (PowerShell, bash, Python); T1203 Exploitation for Client Execution (browser/document exploits); T1047 Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI remote execution); T1053 Scheduled Task/Job. Kill chain relationships: Execution typically follows Initial Access and enables most subsequent tactics — Persistence, Privilege Escalation, Defense Evasion, Discovery, Lateral Movement, Collection, Exfiltration, and C2.

Persistence. Attacker objective: The adversary is trying to maintain their foothold — ensuring continued access even if the system is rebooted, credentials are changed, or initial access is disrupted. Network context: In network telemetry, persistence mechanisms may produce periodic outbound connections to C2 infrastructure, traffic to update or re-establish implants, or unexpected service registrations visible through authentication and directory-service traffic. Representative ATT&CK techniques: T1098 Account Manipulation (adding credentials/keys); T1136 Create Account (creating new accounts for re-entry); T1053 Scheduled Task/Job (recurring execution); T1547 Boot or Logon Autostart Execution; T1505 Server Software Component (web shells). Kill chain relationships: Persistence follows Initial Access and Execution. It ensures the adversary can return to the environment, enabling long-term operations including Discovery, Lateral Movement, and Exfiltration.

Privilege Escalation. Attacker objective: The adversary is trying to gain higher-level permissions — moving from a limited user to admin, root, or SYSTEM privileges to access more resources and bypass restrictions. Network context: In network telemetry, privilege escalation is mostly an endpoint event. Network signs may include authentication traffic with escalated credentials, access to services or shares that were previously inaccessible, or exploitation traffic targeting privilege-related vulnerabilities. Representative ATT&CK techniques: T1068 Exploitation for Privilege Escalation (kernel/service exploits); T1078 Valid Accounts (using higher-privilege credentials); T1548 Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism (UAC bypass, sudo abuse); T1134 Access Token Manipulation. Kill chain relationships: Privilege Escalation often follows Initial Access or Execution and enables Lateral Movement, Collection, and deeper access to sensitive resources.

Defense Evasion. Attacker objective: The adversary is trying to avoid being detected — disabling security tools, obfuscating traffic, clearing logs, or using techniques that blend with normal activity. Network context: In network telemetry, evasion may manifest as encrypted channels to unexpected destinations, traffic on non-standard ports for standard protocols, protocol tunneling, or connections designed to mimic legitimate services. Unusual TCP behavior (mid-stream joins, fragmentation) may also indicate evasion of network-based detection. Representative ATT&CK techniques: T1071 Application Layer Protocol (C2 over HTTP/HTTPS/DNS to blend in); T1573 Encrypted Channel (encrypting C2 to avoid inspection); T1090 Proxy (routing through intermediaries); T1070 Indicator Removal (clearing logs, timestamps); T1036 Masquerading. Kill chain relationships: Defense Evasion operates in parallel with most other tactics — the adversary evades detection throughout the entire operation, from Initial Access through Exfiltration.

Credential Access. Attacker objective: The adversary is trying to steal account credentials — usernames, passwords, tokens, or keys that grant access to systems and data. Network context: In network telemetry, credential access is associated with traffic to authentication-related services and protocols. Repeated access attempts, authentication protocol exchanges, and connections to credential stores or directory services may be relevant indicators. Representative ATT&CK techniques: T1110 Brute Force (password guessing/spraying); T1003 OS Credential Dumping (extracting hashes/tokens); T1557 Adversary-in-the-Middle (intercepting authentication); T1556 Modify Authentication Process; T1555 Credentials from Password Stores. Kill chain relationships: Credential Access often follows Initial Access or Discovery. Successful credential acquisition enables Lateral Movement, Privilege Escalation, and access to protected resources.

Discovery. Attacker objective: The adversary is trying to figure out the environment — mapping internal networks, identifying systems, enumerating accounts, and understanding what resources are available. Network context: In network telemetry, discovery resembles reconnaissance but occurs inside the network after a foothold is established. It involves the adversary's effort to understand the internal environment — identifying reachable systems, available services, accounts, and network topology. Representative ATT&CK techniques: T1046 Network Service Scanning (internal port scanning); T1018 Remote System Discovery (finding other hosts); T1083 File and Directory Discovery; T1069 Permission Groups Discovery; T1016 System Network Configuration Discovery. Kill chain relationships: Discovery follows Initial Access and Execution. It maps the internal environment to support Lateral Movement, Collection, and identification of high-value targets for Exfiltration.

Lateral Movement. Attacker objective: The adversary is trying to move through the environment — pivoting from the initially compromised system to other systems to reach their ultimate target. Network context: In network telemetry, lateral movement appears as internal host-to-host connections using remote-access protocols and file-sharing services. Authentication traffic between internal systems, especially to administrative interfaces, and data transfers between hosts that do not normally communicate are relevant indicators. Representative ATT&CK techniques: T1021 Remote Services (SSH, RDP, SMB/Windows Admin Shares, WinRM); T1080 Taint Shared Content; T1570 Lateral Tool Transfer (copying tools between hosts); T1563 Remote Service Session Hijacking. Kill chain relationships: Lateral Movement follows Discovery and Credential Access. It extends the adversary's reach within the network, enabling Collection, Exfiltration, and Impact on high-value targets.

Collection. Attacker objective: The adversary is gathering and staging data of interest — identifying, aggregating, and preparing files, emails, databases, or other information before exfiltration or further use. The emphasis is on staging: consolidating data in preparation for moving it out. Network context: In network telemetry, collection is associated with access to internal data repositories and the movement of data to staging locations within the network. It is distinct from exfiltration in that the data has not yet left the target environment. Representative ATT&CK techniques: T1560 Archive Collected Data (compressing/encrypting before exfil); T1039 Data from Network Shared Drive; T1114 Email Collection; T1005 Data from Local System; T1119 Automated Collection. Kill chain relationships: Collection follows Discovery and Lateral Movement. It is the preparation step before Exfiltration — the adversary stages the data it intends to steal.

Command and Control. Attacker objective: The adversary is communicating with compromised systems to maintain control — sending commands, receiving data, and managing implants remotely. Network context: In network telemetry, C2 manifests as communication between compromised hosts and external adversary-controlled infrastructure, used for command delivery, status reporting, and information exchange. These connections often use standard protocols to blend with legitimate traffic and may involve encrypted channels to unfamiliar or recently provisioned destinations. Representative ATT&CK techniques: T1071 Application Layer Protocol (HTTP/S, DNS as C2 channel); T1573 Encrypted Channel; T1095 Non-Application Layer Protocol (raw TCP/UDP/ICMP C2); T1090 Proxy (multi-hop C2); T1568 Dynamic Resolution (DGA domains). Kill chain relationships: Command and Control operates throughout the post-exploitation phase — it is the communication backbone enabling Execution, Lateral Movement, Collection, Exfiltration, and Impact.

Exfiltration. Attacker objective: The adversary is stealing data — transferring collected information out of the target network to adversary-controlled infrastructure. Network context: In network telemetry, exfiltration produces outbound transfers with unusually large volume, transfers to uncommon external destinations, or data sent over covert channels (DNS tunneling, steganography, encrypted non-standard ports). Timing may be off-hours to avoid notice. Representative ATT&CK techniques: T1041 Exfiltration Over C2 Channel (using existing C2 connection); T1048 Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol (DNS, ICMP, FTP); T1567 Exfiltration Over Web Service (cloud storage, paste sites); T1029 Scheduled Transfer (timed exfil to avoid detection). Kill chain relationships: Exfiltration follows Collection and often uses the Command and Control channel. It is typically one of the adversary's final objectives, though operations may continue with Impact or further collection.

Impact. Attacker objective: The adversary is trying to disrupt availability, destroy data, or manipulate systems — causing operational damage to the target organization. Network context: In network telemetry, impact is most visible as availability disruption: denial-of-service conditions, loss of connectivity from affected hosts, or propagation traffic associated with destructive payloads spreading through the network. It may also manifest as sudden cessation of normal traffic from compromised systems. Representative ATT&CK techniques: T1498 Network Denial of Service (volumetric/protocol DDoS); T1499 Endpoint Denial of Service (resource exhaustion); T1486 Data Encrypted for Impact (ransomware); T1489 Service Stop; T1565 Data Manipulation. Kill chain relationships: Impact is typically a terminal objective. It may follow any combination of prior tactics, or occur independently (e.g. external DDoS without prior intrusion). Successful impact disrupts business operations and may be the adversary's primary or secondary goal.
